Electrical Signature Analysis

Every AC motor tells you what's wrong. Through its electrical signature.

Current and voltage waveforms captured at the motor control cabinet reveal the condition of the entire drivetrain: motor, coupling, gearbox, and driven load. SAM4 reads those signatures 24/7. No sensors on the machine.

Request a Demo See Detection Capabilities

The Physics

Mechanical faults change the electrical signature of a motor.

Every AC induction motor draws current from the supply. That current creates a rotating magnetic field in the stator, which induces current in the rotor. When something goes wrong mechanically, it shows up in the electrical signal. Here's how.

01

Motor draws current

The motor draws AC current from the supply. The interaction between stator and rotor magnetic fields produces torque that drives the load.

02

Fault changes torque

A worn bearing increases friction. Misalignment creates cyclic load variation. Cavitation alters the hydraulic profile. Each fault changes the torque demand on the motor.

03

Torque changes waveform

The air gap between stator and rotor acts as a transducer. Mechanical changes produce measurable modulations in the current and voltage waveforms.

04

ESA reads the pattern

Each fault type creates a distinct spectral fingerprint. SAM4 reads those patterns continuously from the motor control cabinet. No sensor on the machine.

The core principle: The electrical signature of a motor contains the mechanical condition of the entire drivetrain. You don't need a sensor on the machine. You need a sensor on the electrical supply.

The physics has been validated for over 40 years. What's new is the ability to read these signatures continuously, at scale, with AI models trained on thousands of assets. SAM4 applies it at the motor control cabinet, where current clamps and voltage taps capture the raw waveforms. From there, signal processing and AI do the rest.

Data Acquisition

From current clamp to cloud. No sensors on the machine.

SAM4 captures current and voltage waveforms at the motor control cabinet using non-intrusive current clamps and voltage taps. Sampling rates range from 20 kHz to 64 kHz depending on the asset configuration. Installation takes less than 60 minutes per asset. Motor returns to service the same shift. No scaffolding. No ATEX permits.

Step 01

Current Clamps

Split-core CTs clamp around the motor supply cables in the MCC. Non-intrusive. No electrical disconnection required.

Step 02

DAQ Unit

The Samotics data acquisition unit samples three-phase current and voltage at high frequency. Mounts inside or adjacent to the MCC panel.

Step 03

Cellular Gateway

Data transmits via secure cellular connection. No dependency on plant IT/OT networks. No firewall changes. No VPN configuration.

Step 04

Cloud Processing

Raw waveform data streams to the Samotics cloud platform for signal processing, AI classification, and diagnostic review.

Signal Processing

Turning raw waveforms into fault signatures.

The raw current and voltage signals are time-domain waveforms. To extract fault information, SAM4 converts them to the frequency domain using Fast Fourier Transform (FFT). This produces a spectrum showing the amplitude of each frequency component in the signal.

Mechanical faults produce characteristic patterns in this spectrum. A bearing defect creates sidebands at specific frequencies related to the bearing geometry. Misalignment produces elevated amplitude at 2× running speed, with changes also visible at 1× and 3× harmonics. Cavitation raises the broadband noise floor across 100 Hz to 2 kHz as bubble collapse impulses modulate motor torque. Each fault type has a distinct spectral fingerprint.

What the analysis extracts

  • Rotational speed and load from fundamental frequency
  • Bearing fault frequencies from inner race, outer race, ball pass, and cage defects
  • Misalignment indicators from 1x, 2x, 3x rotational harmonics
  • Rotor bar condition from sideband spacing around supply frequency
  • Stator winding integrity from current unbalance and negative sequence components

How baselines work

SAM4 establishes a spectral baseline for each asset during a calibration period (typically 2 to 4 weeks). This baseline represents the healthy operating condition across all observed load states.

Ongoing monitoring compares new spectra against this baseline. Changes that exceed statistical thresholds trigger anomaly flags. The system accounts for load variation, ambient temperature, and process changes.

AI & Diagnostics

AI detects. Engineers confirm.

Anomaly detection alone generates too many false positives. SAM4 uses a three-stage pipeline: automated detection, AI classification, and human validation. Every alert that reaches your maintenance team has been reviewed by a Samotics reliability engineer.

Stage 01

Anomaly Detection

Statistical models flag deviations from the spectral baseline. Multiple detection algorithms run in parallel to reduce blind spots. Flagged anomalies proceed to classification.

Stage 02

Fault Classification

AI models trained on data from 10,000+ assets classify the anomaly by fault type: bearing, alignment, balance, electrical, or process. The model assigns a severity score and confidence level.

Stage 03

Engineer Validation

A Samotics reliability engineer reviews every classified fault before it reaches your team. They verify the diagnosis, assess urgency, and write a maintenance recommendation in plain language.

What ESA detects

SAM4 identifies developing faults across the full drivetrain: motor, coupling, gearbox, and driven load. Detection lead times range from weeks to months before failure.

Fault type Component Detection method Typical lead time
Mechanical faults
Bearing degradation Motor, gearbox, driven load Characteristic bearing fault frequencies in current spectrum 2 to 5 months
Misalignment Coupling, shaft Changes in 1x, 2x rotational harmonics 4 to 12 weeks
Imbalance Rotor, impeller, fan Elevated 1x rotational component 4 to 12 weeks
Coupling wear Coupling Changes in torsional vibration signature 4 to 12 weeks
Impeller damage Pump, fan, compressor Blade pass frequency shifts, broadband spectral changes Weeks to months
Rotor eccentricity Motor, ESP Elevated slip-frequency sidebands around supply frequency Weeks to months
Belt / drive slip Belt drive, VFD-driven load Loss of expected load transfer, speed-torque mismatch Days to weeks
Electrical faults
Winding insulation breakdown Stator Current unbalance, negative sequence component analysis Weeks to months
Broken rotor bars Rotor Sideband frequencies around supply frequency Weeks to months
Supply power quality Electrical supply, VFD Voltage/current unbalance, harmonic distortion, overcurrent events Real-time
Process & operational
Cavitation Pump impeller Broadband noise increase, load instability patterns Days to weeks
Pump blockages Pump Sudden load spike or sustained overload pattern in current signature Near-instant
Clogging / fouling Pump, filter, screen Progressive load increase, efficiency degradation Days to weeks
Dry running Pump Sudden load drop, current signature collapse below normal operating range Near-instant
Process deviations Driven load Load profile changes, operating point drift Real-time

This table covers the most common fault types. ESA detects additional conditions depending on equipment type, operating context, and load profile.

Real detections. Named customers.

Documented fault detections from production deployments. Each validated by post-maintenance inspection.

ArcelorMittal

Bearing fault detected 5 months early

SAM4 identified developing bearing degradation in a critical drive motor at a rolling mill. The fault was confirmed during a planned maintenance window. Without the early warning, the failure would have caused an unplanned production stop.

5 months advance warning before failure

Schiphol Airport

Gearbox fault missed by vibration, caught by ESA

SAM4 detected bearing damage in a baggage handling gearbox motor that vibration monitoring had not flagged. Across the deployment, SAM4 achieved a 100% detection rate on 9 confirmed faults.

9/9 faults detected (100% accuracy)

Yorkshire Water

70,000 manual inspection trips eliminated per year

Yorkshire Water deployed SAM4 across submersible pumps at remote pumping stations. ESA replaced physical inspection rounds with continuous remote monitoring, cutting site visits by 70,000 annually and catching a single fault that prevented a £390K pollution fine.

70,000 inspection trips replaced per year

DuPont

€1.7M in costs prevented over three years

DuPont deployed SAM4 on critical rotating equipment across multiple sites. Over three years, the system identified faults that would have resulted in unplanned shutdowns. Total prevented cost: €1.7 million. Payback achieved within 8 months.

€1.7M costs prevented over 3 years
"At first I was a bit sceptical about SAM4's ability to detect failures by analysing electrical waveforms, but the results speak for themselves: it just works."
Philip Curran, DuPont

Explore all customer results →

Where ESA works. And where it doesn't.

No monitoring technology covers everything. Stating the boundaries builds trust.

Where ESA works

✓

AC induction motors

Fixed-speed and VFD-driven. Both fully supported.

✓

5 Hz and above

Standard 50/60 Hz mains-fed and VFD output frequencies above 5 Hz.

✓

Motor running under load

Intermittent duty cycles supported with minimum run-time thresholds.

✓

Access to MCC cabinet

Current clamps install at the motor control cabinet. No access to the machine needed.

✓

Complements existing vibration

Keep vibration where it's installed. ESA covers the assets vibration cannot reach.

Where ESA doesn't apply

✗

DC motors, steppers, servos

ESA requires AC induction motor physics. Other motor types are not supported.

✗

VFD output below 5 Hz

Spectral analysis needs sufficient electrical frequency to resolve fault patterns.

✗

Very short run times (<10 seconds)

Intermittent duty is fine. But extremely short cycles limit analysis depth.

✗

No MCC access

Direct bus-bar connections or inaccessible cabinets may prevent installation.

✗

Calibration period: 2–4 weeks

SAM4 needs a baseline to learn normal operation. No alerts during this period.

Validated science. Proven at scale.

ESA is not experimental. The physics is well-established. The deployment is global.

40+ years of ESA research and validation
10,000+ assets monitored globally
80+ customers across 5 continents
>90% fault detection rate

See what your electrical data reveals.

Request a demo and speak with a reliability engineer within 48 hours. No sales pitch. A technical conversation about your assets.
